metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend `_is_cas_mismatch` (or the proposed `_cas_once` helper) to recognize a `git push --force-with-lease` exit-128 lease violation as a CAS mismatch in addition to the existing `git update-ref` exit-128 check — both command shapes must be classified correctly.
findings.finding: The `_is_cas_mismatch` discriminator (line 425) checks `'update-ref' in args` to identify CAS mismatches. The plan's generalization adds a `ref_name` parameter — but when `remote` is given, the CAS is performed by `git push --force-with-lease`, not `git update-ref`. A lease-violation from `git push` exits 128 but `exc.cmd` will contain `['git', 'push', ...]`, not `update-ref`. The generalized discriminator will therefore fail to recognize a remote-path CAS mismatch: `_cas_once` will re-raise as a non-mismatch `CalledProcessError` instead of returning `False`, so `acquire` will surface a raw `CalledProcessError` rather than the specified `RefLockHeldError`. The plan specifies threading a `ref_name` param through the discriminator but does not specify that the discriminator must also accept the command shape (`update-ref` vs `push`) as a classifying dimension.
findings.location: Design decisions §1 and §2; AC 'The CAS reuses `_cas_advance_with_retry` / `_is_cas_mismatch`'; `_advisory_lock.py:415-426`
findings.suggested_fix: In `_cas_once`, detect the CAS failure by command shape: for `update-ref` commands use the existing `_is_cas_mismatch` logic; for `push` commands, detect exit-128 and check `exc.stderr` for git's lease-rejection phrase (e.g., `'stale info'` or `'rejected'` with `'--force-with-lease'`). Or, unify: replace the `'update-ref' in args` guard with a broader shape-plus-exit-code check that covers both code paths.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the `_is_cas_mismatch` / `_cas_once` specification to explicitly handle the remote-path CAS command (`git push --force-with-lease`): either define a separate discriminator for push-exit-128 (e.g., checking `'push' in args and exc.returncode == 128`) or document that `_cas_once` uses two discriminators — one for local `update-ref` and one for remote `push` — co-located with the `_cas_once` definition in Design Decision 2.
findings.finding: `_is_cas_mismatch` (line 415–426) checks `'update-ref' in args` as the command-shape discriminator. The plan proposes adding a `ref_name` parameter to generalize it — but when `remote` is given, the CAS is done via `git push --force-with-lease` (not `git update-ref`), so `'update-ref' in args` will NEVER match a remote push command. The `ref_name` parameter fix is therefore incomplete: remote-path CAS mismatches will always return False from `_is_cas_mismatch`, making `_cas_once` unable to distinguish a CAS mismatch from a genuine push failure on the remote path.
findings.location: Design Decision 1 (`_is_cas_mismatch` seam); Design Decision 5 (remote CAS via `git push --force-with-lease`); `_advisory_lock.py:424-426`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit C1 integration AC (or note in AC0) that calls `_ref_lock.acquire(ref='refs/reconciler/lock', remote='origin')` directly and verifies the push–fetch cycle through the module's own subprocess invocations, or explicitly document the deferral to a named child with rationale.
findings.finding: The `_ref_lock.py` module's remote-path functions (acquire/release/read with `remote='origin'`) are never exercised end-to-end through the module's own API in C1 — AC0 only proves the git refspec round-trips at the shell level, not that the module's `push --force-with-lease` / `fetch` invocations in the acquire/release code paths behave correctly over the live GitHub remote. The plan defers reconciler wiring to C3 but does not explicitly state that the module-level remote integration test is also deferred.
findings.location: AC0; Design decision 5 ('remote path'); Acceptance Criteria — second bullet

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC0's proving command/test to explicitly verify the CAS rejection path: push with a stale blob-oid expectation must fail with a distinguishable (non-zero, non-timeout) error, and document the exact exit code / stderr pattern that `_ref_lock.py` must treat as a CAS mismatch.
findings.finding: `git push --force-with-lease=<ref>:<blob-oid>` with a blob OID (not a commit SHA) as the expected value is an unverified capability assertion. `--force-with-lease` is specified and implemented primarily for branch/commit refs; its behavior when the lease OID is a raw blob object is not documented and may not work as expected on GitHub's receive-pack. AC0 is scoped to proving push+fetch round-trip feasibility, but does not explicitly call out verifying the CAS semantic (that a push with the wrong blob-oid expectation is rejected with a non-zero exit that signals the CAS mismatch). These are two distinct capabilities.
findings.location: Design decisions §5 (Distributed CAS via optional remote); AC0
findings.suggested_fix: In the AC0 spike script, after a successful acquire (push blob-oid B1), attempt a second push with `--force-with-lease=refs/reconciler/lock:000...0` (wrong oid) and confirm it exits non-zero; record the exit code and stderr substring in the ADR so `_ref_lock.py`'s error classifier is empirically grounded.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC0 to explicitly prove the `--force-with-lease=<ref>:<blob-oid>` CAS update (not just push+fetch creation) through the GitHub remote, so the full acquire→release round-trip over the remote is verified by the spike.
findings.finding: AC0's stated scope ('push + fetch through the origin GitHub remote') does not explicitly include proving the remote CAS step — `git push --force-with-lease=<ref>:<old-blob-oid>` where the lease OID resolves to a blob rather than a commit. GitHub's receive-pack could accept the ref→blob push but still reject the lease-form update if its object-type validation differs for blob OIDs. This leaves the remote CAS mechanism unverified by the spike.
findings.location: AC0 / Design decisions §5 / What section (remote path: 'git push --force-with-lease=<ref>:<old-oid>')
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC0's proving command/test: after the initial push, re-push with `git push --force-with-lease=refs/reconciler/lock-test:<original-blob-oid> origin refs/reconciler/lock-test` (updating to a new blob) and verify it succeeds; also verify that a stale-oid push is rejected with the expected exit code. Record the result in the ADR.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the explicit contract for `read` when the ref does not exist (e.g. return `None` to signal 'free', or raise a typed `RefLockAbsentError`) and add it to Design decision 3 and the corresponding AC unit-test scenario.
findings.finding: The contract for `read` on an absent ref is unspecified: the plan defines fail-closed behavior for corrupt/empty blobs (raise `RefLockCorruptError`, treat as HELD) but never states what `read` returns or raises when the ref does not exist at all — which is the normal 'unlocked' state. The AC line says '(defined contract)' implying the contract exists somewhere, but no definition is given in the plan body or Design decisions.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'read on present vs absent ref (defined contract)'; Design decision 3
findings.suggested_fix: In Design decision 3, add: 'When the ref does not exist, `read` returns `None` (lock is free); this is distinct from a corrupt blob (lock state unknown = HELD). The unit test for absent-ref verifies `read` returns `None` and that acquire on a None-state ref succeeds.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `read` contract for an absent ref (ref does not exist) in the plan body — e.g. returns `None` (lock free) or raises a distinct `RefLockNotHeldError` — and confirm whether 'absent' is fail-open (free) or fail-closed (treated as held), so the unit-test AC has an unambiguous contract to verify.
findings.finding: The AC for unit tests references 'read on present vs absent ref (defined contract)' but the plan body never specifies what `read` returns or raises when the ref does not exist. The What section covers corrupt/empty/partial blob -> RefLockCorruptError, but a missing ref is a distinct state (no bytes at all vs empty bytes). An implementer writing the absent-ref test case has no specified contract to verify against.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — unit tests bullet: 'read on present vs absent ref (defined contract)'; also What section blob/read description
findings.suggested_fix:
Add one sentence to the What section or DD3: 'If the ref does not exist, `read` returns `None` (the lock is free); only a present but unparseable blob raises `RefLockCorruptError`.' Then update the AC test bullet to 'read on an absent ref returns None (lock free); read on a present ref returns the parsed blob dict.'

Alternatively, if fail-closed is the right default everywhere, state: '`read` on an absent ref raises `RefLockCorruptError` with a sentinel message; callers treat it as HELD.' Either is fine — the plan must pick one.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define a catch-all `RefLockGitError` (or similar) wrapping unexpected `CalledProcessError` from git operations, document its fail-closed treatment (= HELD / cannot determine state) alongside `RefLockTimeoutError` in the AC and ADR, and ensure `_cas_once` / the acquire/release wrappers surface it consistently.
findings.finding: Non-timeout, non-CAS-mismatch subprocess failures (e.g. DNS failure, permission error, or non-128 git exit during push/fetch) propagate as raw CalledProcessError, but the plan specifies no caller contract for this exception class — callers are only told to treat RefLockCorruptError and RefLockTimeoutError as HELD, leaving an unhandled failure mode that could crash the reconciler rather than failing closed.
findings.location: Design decisions §5 (remote CAS path), §6 (timeouts), AC: 'RefLockTimeoutError (treated as HELD)'
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap non-mismatch CalledProcessError in a new RefLockGitError in _cas_once (or in the acquire/release entry points), add it to the AC's 'treated as HELD' list alongside RefLockTimeoutError, and add a unit-test case for 'git push returns exit 1 (non-128)' verifying fail-closed behavior.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the explicit contract for `read` on an absent ref in the Design Decisions section (e.g., return `None` meaning 'unlocked', or raise a named exception), and update the AC to assert that specific behavior rather than deferring to 'defined contract'.
findings.finding: The `read` contract for an absent ref (ref does not exist) is listed as a test case ('read on present vs absent ref (defined contract)') but the plan never defines what the expected behavior is — should `read` return `None`, raise `RefLockCorruptError`, raise a distinct `RefLockAbsentError`, or raise `KeyError`? Shipping the test with a vague 'defined contract' risks the test asserting whatever the implementation happens to do on first write, becoming a change-detector.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — 'read on present vs absent ref (defined contract)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Design Decision 3 (or a new DD): '`read` on a ref that does not exist returns `None` (meaning: no lock held); callers may treat `None` as free. `RefLockCorruptError` is reserved for refs that exist but whose blob cannot be parsed. These are distinct states and must be handled separately by callers.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify in the plan body (e.g., under Design decisions §3 or the What section) what `read` returns or raises when the ref does not exist — e.g., `returns None` (lock free) vs `raises RefLockAbsentError` — so the AC's 'defined contract' is actually defined.
findings.finding: The plan never defines the `read` contract for an absent ref — i.e., what `read` returns or raises when `refs/reconciler/lock` does not exist at all. The AC says 'read on present vs absent ref (defined contract)' but the plan body specifies fail-closed behavior only for corrupt/partial/empty blobs (`RefLockCorruptError`). Callers of `read` need a defined contract for the absent case to implement correct 'is the lock free?' logic; without it, an implementer could plausibly raise `RefLockCorruptError` (treating absent as corrupt, permanently blocking), return `None` (treating absent as free), or raise a distinct `RefLockAbsentError`. Each choice produces different caller behavior for the acquire precondition check.
findings.location: AC 'Unit tests cover: … `read` on present vs absent ref (defined contract)'; What section 'read'; Design decisions §3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Design Decision 3 (or the `read` description in the What section): 'When the ref does not exist, `read` returns `None` (lock is free); callers distinguish `None` (absent = free) from `RefLockCorruptError` (present but unreadable = treat as held).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit `read`-absent-ref contract to the plan (e.g., Design Decision 3 or a new DD): specify whether `read` returns `None` (ref free), raises `RefLockCorruptError`, or raises a dedicated `RefLockAbsentError`, and update the AC to reference that decision rather than leaving it open.
findings.finding: `read`'s return/raise contract for an absent ref (ref does not exist) is named as a test case in the ACs ('read on present vs absent ref (defined contract)') but the contract is never defined anywhere in the plan body or Design Decisions. An implementor cannot write the test or the implementation without independently deciding whether `read` returns None, raises KeyError, raises RefLockCorruptError, or raises a new exception.
findings.location: AC: 'Unit tests cover: read on present vs absent ref (defined contract)'; What section — `read` bullet; Design Decisions 3–5 (none cover absent-ref case)
findings.suggested_fix: In Design Decision 3, add: 'When the ref does not exist, `read` returns `None` (the ref is free, not HELD). Only corrupt-but-present blobs trigger fail-closed treatment.' This aligns with the acquire-on-free-ref test case and makes the absent/free distinction explicit.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one subprocess-mocked unit test that calls `acquire`/`release` with `remote='origin'` and asserts the correct `git fetch` + `git push --force-with-lease=<ref>:<oid>` subprocess arguments are issued, even if the subprocess is patched to return success — this keeps the command-construction logic under test without requiring a live remote.
findings.finding: The `remote` code path in `_ref_lock.py` (the `git fetch` + `git push --force-with-lease` branch, triggered when `remote != None`) ships in C1 but the listed unit tests explicitly use `remote=None` for all cases. Even a subprocess-mocked unit test exercising `acquire(..., remote='origin')` is absent. The plan defers live integration to C3, but the module-level branching logic for the remote path goes untested — a typo or wrong flag in the push invocation would pass the entire test suite.
findings.location: Design Decision 5 ('When remote is None (unit tests / local)…'); Acceptance Criteria — unit test list

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether `_ref_lock.py` will use `rebar._store.gitutil.run_git` (which already supports `timeout=`) for its subprocess calls, rather than introducing yet another private git wrapper — and if `gitutil.run_git` is unsuitable for the reconciler package (e.g. import boundary), document why and add that rationale to the ADR.
findings.finding: The plan introduces new git subprocess calls in `_ref_lock.py` without mentioning `src/rebar/_store/gitutil.run_git`, the project-wide git wrapper created explicitly to consolidate drifted private git helpers; a new file shelling out to git is likely to add a third private wrapper alongside `_advisory_lock._git_run` and `_advisory_lock._git_run_in` (which themselves already have no timeout and were not consolidated into `gitutil`).
findings.location: Plan body: 'Every `subprocess.run` in `_ref_lock.py` sets `timeout=`'; Design decisions §6

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to AC0's scope (or the ADR) an explicit verification that a pushed `refs/reconciler/*` blob ref survives GitHub's GC / idle-period handling — e.g., confirm the blob is still fetchable 24–48 hours after push, or confirm GitHub's documented GC SLA for ref-reachable objects covers non-standard ref namespaces.
findings.finding: The plan does not address GitHub server-side garbage collection safety for refs that point directly at blob objects in a non-standard namespace (`refs/reconciler/*`). Git's object GC considers an object reachable if a ref points to it; however, GitHub may run proprietary GC or pack-refs processes that do not trace through custom non-`refs/heads/*` / non-`refs/tags/*` refs in the same way. A blob silently collected between acquire and release would produce a corrupt lock state with no warning.
findings.location: What section — 'Ref → blob, so it never lives in the tickets working tree and is never union-merged'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to AC0's proving test a dwell/GC check or document in the ADR that GitHub's GC preserves ref-reachable blobs under `refs/reconciler/*` (or confirm the fallback commit path is preferred precisely because commits are GC-safe by design).
findings.finding: The plan assumes GitHub's GC infrastructure will retain a loose blob object that is ref-reachable only via a custom `refs/reconciler/*` ref (i.e., not reachable via any commit's tree). Standard git guarantees ref-reachability prevents GC, but GitHub runs GC on its own schedule and its behavior for non-branch/non-tag refs pointing directly at blobs is unverified. If GitHub treats custom refs as 'weak' for GC purposes, the lock blob could be silently collected mid-hold.
findings.location: What section ('Ref -> blob, so it never lives in the tickets working tree') / AC0 / Design Decision 7 (fallback)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that `_cas_advance_with_retry` must also gain a `ref_name` parameter (forwarded from caller to its internal `_is_cas_mismatch(exc, ref_name=ref_name)` call at line 469) — Design Decision 1 currently describes only updating the `_is_cas_mismatch` signature and line 585, silently omitting the intermediate signature change on `_cas_advance_with_retry` itself.
findings.finding: Design Decision 1 says 'update the one external call site (`_advisory_lock.py:585`) to pass `ref_name="refs/heads/tickets"` explicitly', but line 585 calls `_cas_advance_with_retry(repo_root, _mutate_and_advance)` — not `_is_cas_mismatch`. Only `_is_cas_mismatch` is planned to gain a `ref_name` parameter, and it is invoked at line 469 *inside* `_cas_advance_with_retry` as `_is_cas_mismatch(exc)` (no ref name). For `_ref_lock.py` to supply its ref name to the mismatch classifier, `_cas_advance_with_retry` must also accept and forward `ref_name` to its internal call at line 469. The plan conflates these two call sites and does not specify this forwarding, leaving the seam incomplete.
findings.location: Design decisions § 1 ('`_is_cas_mismatch` seam (backward-compatible)') and AC line: 'The CAS reuses `_cas_advance_with_retry` / `_is_cas_mismatch` … via the `_cas_once` seam'
findings.suggested_fix: In Design Decision 1, add: '_cas_advance_with_retry gains a `ref_name: str = "refs/heads/tickets"` parameter and passes it through to `_is_cas_mismatch(exc, ref_name=ref_name)` at line 469. The sole call site at line 585 passes `ref_name="refs/heads/tickets"` explicitly; `_ref_lock.py` passes its configured ref name.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the structured-log requirement (and the corresponding AC) to cover RefLockTimeoutError and unexpected subprocess failures, emitting at minimum: the ref name, operation (acquire/release), error type, and git stderr snippet.
findings.finding: Structured log emission is specified only for 'acquire-already-held' and 'stale-oid release', but RefLockTimeoutError and unexpected subprocess failures (RefLockGitError / CalledProcessError) — which are the most operationally alarming failure modes — have no specified log/metric/trace instrumentation, leaving a blind spot for on-call operators.
findings.location: What section: 'Add structured log emission for acquire-failure (already held) and release on a stale oid'; AC: 'Structured logs are emitted for acquire-already-held and stale-oid release'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Expose timeout values as keyword parameters (with the specified defaults) on `acquire`, `release`, and `read`, or as module-level constants overridable via environment variables (e.g., `REBAR_REF_LOCK_LOCAL_TIMEOUT_S`, `REBAR_REF_LOCK_REMOTE_TIMEOUT_S`), and document the override mechanism in the ADR.
findings.finding: Subprocess timeout values (5 s local / 30 s remote) are specified as hardcoded constants in the implementation rather than configurable parameters, making them impossible to tune per deployment environment (e.g., a slow CI runner, a corporate Git proxy, or a self-hosted GitHub Enterprise instance with higher latency) without a code change.
findings.location: Design decisions §6: 'Every subprocess.run in _ref_lock.py sets timeout= — 5 s for local object/ref ops, 30 s for remote push/fetch'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `local_timeout: float = 5.0, remote_timeout: float = 30.0` kwargs to acquire/release/read (passed through to subprocess calls). This preserves defaults, keeps existing callers unchanged, and allows per-invocation or per-deployment tuning.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm that `_cas_once` and the generalized `_is_cas_mismatch` have ≥3 justified call sites in C1+C2 combined, OR replace the shared-helper approach with a small self-contained CAS primitive directly in `_ref_lock.py` (the tickets-branch and ref-lock paths have different retry contracts and sharing them yields premature abstraction at 2 call sites).
findings.finding: `_cas_once` is a new abstraction with only 2 total call sites (the existing `_cas_advance_with_retry` loop + the new `_ref_lock.py` acquire/release), which is below the Rule-of-Three threshold; the refactor also conflates fundamentally different retry semantics (retry-on-race vs. fail-immediately-on-held) into one helper, increasing coupling without a justified third consumer.
findings.location: Design decisions §2; plan body: 'Factor a `_cas_once(...) -> bool` helper'
findings.suggested_fix: In `_ref_lock.py`, implement a standalone `_ref_cas(repo_root, ref, new_oid, old_oid) -> bool` (True = success, False = old-oid mismatch, raises on other errors) using a direct `subprocess.run(['git', 'update-ref', ref, new_oid, old_oid], ...)` with `check=False`. This avoids touching `_advisory_lock.py` at all for C1 and leaves the C4 deduplication decision to when a genuine third consumer exists.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document in the ADR why ref→blob is preferred over ref→tiny-commit as the primary path (e.g., smaller object, simpler `read` path, no commit-tree overhead) so the design choice is justified rather than implicit, and confirm the preference survives AC0's empirical result.
findings.finding: The plan chooses ref→blob as the primary design over the more conventional ref→tiny-commit without documenting the justification for that preference. The ref→tiny-commit path (AC0 fallback, Design decision §7) is equally workable — it avoids GC ambiguity, uses only commit objects (which all git tooling handles uniformly), and `git hash-object -w` + a synthetic tree + `git commit-tree` is a well-trodden pattern. The blob-first choice is novel enough to require a rationale, but the plan only says 'Ref → blob, so it never lives in the tickets working tree' — which applies equally to a ref→tiny-commit.
findings.location: What section — first bullet; Design decision §7
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Alternatives considered' section to the ADR: ref→tiny-commit (conventional, GC-safe, universal tooling) vs ref→blob (smaller, simpler read via `git cat-file blob <ref>` directly). Document which specific property of blob makes it preferable; if none exists, consider making tiny-commit the primary and blob the optimisation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for the `_advisory_lock.py` changes (e.g., 'revert commits A–B to restore pre-refactor state; `_ref_lock.py` addition is independently revertable') and consider an expand-contract sequence: introduce `_cas_once` as an additive internal helper validated by existing tests before wiring `_ref_lock.py` on top of it.
findings.finding: The plan modifies the shared, production-path module `_advisory_lock.py` (adding a parameter to `_is_cas_mismatch` and extracting an internal `_cas_once` helper) but specifies no rollback or back-out plan if those changes introduce a regression in the existing ticket-lock path.
findings.location: Design decisions §1–2; AC line 'The CAS reuses `_cas_advance_with_retry` / `_is_cas_mismatch` (`_advisory_lock.py:447-487,415`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Split the change into two independently-revertable commits: (1) add `_cas_once` as a private helper inside `_advisory_lock.py` and update `_cas_advance_with_retry` to call it — existing tests prove no regression; (2) add `_ref_lock.py` importing `_cas_once`/`_is_cas_mismatch`. Document in the ADR: 'to roll back, revert commit (2) alone; to roll back the refactor, revert commit (1) — `_ref_lock.py` must be reverted first'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assess whether `fence` should be omitted from the C1 blob schema entirely (added in C2 when steal logic is implemented) to avoid shipping dead validation and test surface; if it must be present in C1 for forward-compatibility, document the forward-compat rationale explicitly in the ADR.
findings.finding: The `fence` field (written as 0 on acquire, validated in `RefLockCorruptError` checks) has no consumer in C1 — all increment/renew/steal logic is explicitly deferred to C2 — making it YAGNI schema surface in the C1 deliverable, including dead validation branches in `read` and test coverage for a field with no observable behavior.
findings.location: Plan body: 'fence is a monotonic progress-witness / generation counter (seeded to 0 on first acquire; C2 increments it on renew/steal)'; Design decisions §4; AC (corrupt conditions list)

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
